title: Why identical cells differ
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: Genes don't transcribe in a smooth stream — they fire in bursts. A promoter flickers between OFF and ON, and while it's ON a flurry of mRNAs is made, then decays. Because molecule counts are small and every reaction fires at a random time, the Gillespie algorithm (exact stochastic simulation of the chemical master equation) shows protein levels jumping and sagging. Run it again with a different seed and you get a different cell — genetically identical, yet noticeably unlike. This intrinsic noise is why a clonal population is never truly uniform.
